ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Central venous pressure (CVP)**, **mean arterial pressure (MAP)**, and **pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP)** help nurses interpret **preload**, **afterload**, and **pump function** in critically ill clients. CVP approximates **right-sided filling** when measured at end-expiration with a calibrated transducer; MAP estimates **organ perfusion pressure** and is a common resuscitation target; PAWP reflects **left-sided filling pressures** when a pulmonary artery catheter is in place and the balloon is wedged per protocol. Canadian stems may show **kPa** for pressures in some contexts, but NCLEX-RN prioritization hinges on **trend interpretation**, **artifact avoidance**, and **knowing what each number cannot tell you alone**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items reward **trending** over single snapshots, **levelling and zeroing** themes, and **first actions** when a line disconnects or dampens. Traps include treating CVP as a perfect volume number, ignoring **valve disease** or **ventricular interdependence**, or choosing **large fluid boluses** solely because CVP is “low” when the client has **end-stage right heart failure** or **tamponade** physiology in the stem.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **CVP**: influenced by **PEEP**, **intra-abdominal pressure**, **tricuspid disease**, and **respiratory variation** — interpret with the whole exam.
- **MAP**: roughly **(SBP + 2×DBP)/3**; used with **urine output** and **mentation** to judge perfusion.
- **PAWP**: obtained only with **provider orders** and **waveform confirmation**; never inflate the balloon longer than protocol allows.
- **Nursing priorities**: maintain **sterile dressings**, **stopcock integrity**, **alarm limits**, and **clear handoff** of numeric baselines after repositioning.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 67-year-old client post–**mitral valve repair** has a **PA catheter** and arterial line. After turning, the nurse notices the **arterial waveform is dampened** and **MAP** reads **58 mmHg**, while **CVP** rose from **8 to 14 mmHg** with unchanged **PAWP** on the screen capture you are shown.

**Fork:** Your **first** actions cluster around **assessing line patency**, **re-levelling** transducers if indicated, **comparing** to non-invasive BP, and **notifying** the provider with **objective data** — not bolusing fluid based on one CVP value alone.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Trend beats snapshot** for all invasive pressures.
- **Waveform quality** is part of assessment — dampening can hide real hypotension.
- **Synthesis:** Pair invasive numbers with **perfusion** (mentation, UO, lactate themes when shown) before choosing aggressive fluids.

**Related:** [heart failure priorities](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy) · [shock recognition](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Which principle best fits NCLEX-RN prioritization for Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP?
options: Complete routine documentation before notifying the provider when vitals cross critical thresholds
Assess objective perfusion and oxygenation trends, notify the provider when indicated, and prevent harm before routine tasks
Delegate all unstable assessments to the nursing assistant to save time
Encourage ambulation first to rule out anxiety-related symptoms
preTest.rationale: NCLEX-RN rewards assessment of unstable findings, timely communication, and safety sequencing over documentation, delegation of RN-level judgment, or routine tasks when risk is rising.

preTest.question: A client shows subtle deterioration related to Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP. What is the safest immediate focus?
options: Deep discharge teaching about medications
Focused reassessment of airway, breathing, circulation, and mentation with prepared escalation
Asking the family to leave so the client can rest
Holding all medications until the physician rounds in the morning
preTest.rationale: When deterioration is possible, nurses prioritize focused reassessment and prepared escalation per orders and policy—not delayed teaching, family removal as a primary intervention, or blanket medication holds without orders.

postTest.question: Which finding should prompt the fastest escalation when linked to Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP?
options: A single benign transient beep on a pulse oximeter with immediate recovery
Hypotension with altered mentation or ischemic chest pain alongside new rhythm or ST changes
A patient requesting an extra pillow for comfort
A stable chronic edema without new respiratory symptoms
postTest.rationale: Hypotension with altered mentation or ischemic symptoms plus new rhythm or ST changes signals imminent harm and requires urgent assessment, monitoring, and provider activation per protocol.

postTest.question: Teaching for Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP is appropriate when which condition is met?
options: When the client is hypoxic, confused, and hypotensive
After stabilization when the client is alert, understands, and perfusion is adequate
Only at discharge regardless of stability
Before any assessment to save time
postTest.rationale: Education is prioritized after life threats are stabilized and the patient can participate safely—never before addressing airway, breathing, circulation, and urgent ischemia or shock patterns.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Arterial lines** provide **continuous beat-to-beat** blood pressure monitoring and **reliable sampling** for **ABGs** in unstable clients. Nursing responsibilities include **insertion assist** per scope, **securement**, **dressing integrity**, **zeroing** the transducer at the **phlebostatic axis**, **alarm management**, and **recognizing complications** (ischemia, thrombosis, infection, hemorrhage from disconnect). For NCLEX-RN, distinguish **artifact** (kinked tubing, air bubbles, patient movement) from **true hypotension** before escalating therapy.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards test **first-line safety**: **stop bleeding** if a line disconnects, **activate help** for sudden hemorrhage, **compare** invasive and non-invasive pressures when waveforms look wrong, and **avoid** wrist flexion injury in radial sites. Expect questions on **Allen test** themes when radial access is planned and **neurovascular checks** for distal perfusion after placement.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Monitoring bundle**: continuous waveform when ordered, **q-shift** site checks, **mark insertion** date, **closed** sampling system.
- **Complications**: **distal ischemia** (pallor, pain, weak pulse), **infection** at hub/dressing, **hematoma**.
- **Overdamped vs underdamped** waveforms change numeric display — reassess setup before treating numbers.
- **Scope:** nurses **do not** independently remove arterial lines unless protocol explicitly allows; follow orders for discontinuation.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 54-year-old client in **ICU** has a **radial arterial line**. The nurse notes **bright red blood** pulsing from a **loose connection** at the stopcock while the monitor alarms **MAP 40**.

**Fork:** **Immediate patient safety** — **tighten/close** the system per training, **apply pressure** if external bleeding, **call for help**, and **notify** the provider while monitoring **distal perfusion**. Do not leave to **complete charting** first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Disconnect = bleeding risk** until proven otherwise.
- **Waveform troubleshooting** precedes blind fluid pushes.

**Related:** [hemodynamic monitoring](LESSON:hemodynamic-monitoring-cvp-map-pawp) · [shock](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [US hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Cardiac output (CO)** is **stroke volume × heart rate** and represents **minute delivery** of blood to tissues. **Stroke volume** depends on **preload**, **afterload**, and **contractility**. NCLEX-RN frames these relationships for **heart failure**, **shock**, and **arrhythmia** vignettes where the nurse must connect **tachycardia** compensating for low SV, or **bradycardia** collapsing CO despite “normal” squeeze.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners love **compensation limits**: HR can only maintain CO until **diastolic filling** or **ischemia** breaks the cycle. They test **assessment cues** (JVD, lung congestion, cool extremities) rather than asking you to solve thermodilution arithmetic.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Preload**: volume returning to the ventricle — **too low** → hypoperfusion; **too high** → congestion in HF.
- **Afterload**: resistance the ventricle must overcome — **high SVR** can drop SV when contractility is fixed.
- **Contractility**: weakened in **ischemia**, **sepsis late**, **negative inotropes**.
- **Nursing actions**: strict **I&O**, **daily weights**, **telemetry**, **orthostatic vitals** when appropriate, and **medication timing** for afterload reducers per orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **acute HF** has **HR 128** in **sinus tachycardia**, **BP 92/60**, and **cool fingers** with **UOP 15 mL/hr** for two hours.

**Fork:** Think **low perfusion with compensatory tachycardia** — prioritize **assessment**, **notify provider**, **prepare** for ordered diuretics/inotropes/nitrates per pathway, and **avoid** strenuous ambulation.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **HR is not “just anxiety”** when perfusion is failing.
- **SV × HR** helps you explain why **new AFib** can crash CO.

**Related:** [AFib rate control](LESSON:atrial-fibrillation-rate-control) · [HF priorities](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂)
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO₂)** reflects the **balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption** in the pulmonary artery sample. **Central venous ScvO₂** (from a central line) is a related surrogate used in some sepsis bundles. A **falling** value can signal **low delivery** (anemia, hypoxia, low CO) or **high consumption** (fever, shivering, stress) — the nurse integrates **SpO₂**, **hemoglobin**, **perfusion**, and **lactate** themes when the stem provides them.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **interpretation**, not memorizing a single threshold for every device. Expect **pre-analytic** errors (draw from wrong port) and **clinical integration** questions where **SaO₂ is high** but **delivery is still poor** because hemoglobin or CO is inadequate.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Delivery components**: **SaO₂**, **Hb**, **cardiac output**.
- **Consumption**: sepsis, pain, fever — can lower saturation of venous blood even when lungs look “fine.”
- **Nursing care**: label **line ports**, draw from **correct lumens**, trend values, and communicate **sudden** drops.
- **Safety:** never treat a number in isolation — **examine the patient**.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** After **major surgery**, a client’s **ScvO₂** drops from **74% to 62%** with **rising lactate**, **HR 118**, and **BP trending down**.

**Fork:** Treat as **supply/demand mismatch** — **notify provider**, **increase monitoring**, **review** bleeding, **fluid responsiveness** per orders, and **oxygen** therapy per protocol.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Falling SvO₂/ScvO₂ + lactate** is an escalation flag.
- **High SpO₂** does not guarantee adequate **delivery**.

**Related:** [shock recognition](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [sepsis gold](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-gold).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Heart Blocks: First Degree, Mobitz I, Mobitz II, Third Degree
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Heart Blocks: First Degree, Mobitz I, Mobitz II, Third Degree | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Heart Blocks: First Degree, Mobitz I, Mobitz II, Third Degree — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **AV blocks** describe **delayed or interrupted** conduction between atria and ventricles. **First-degree** PR prolongation is usually stable monitoring. **Mobitz I (Wenckebach)** often has **benign progression patterns** on monitored floors but still requires **trend review**. **Mobitz II** and **third-degree (complete) heart block** are **high-risk** for **sudden bradycardia** and **hypoperfusion** and frequently require **pacing** or **medication** per ACLS-aligned orders.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items discriminate **stable vs unstable bradycardia**: hypotension, altered mentation, pulmonary edema, ischemic chest pain → **atropine/transcutaneous pacing** themes and **rapid communication**. Do not confuse **Mobitz I** with **Mobitz II** when the stem shows **constant PR** with **sudden dropped QRS**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Mobitz I**: **lengthening PR** then dropped beat.
- **Mobitz II**: **constant PR** with intermittent non-conducted P waves — **risk of complete block**.
- **Third-degree**: **AV dissociation** — P waves march through independent of QRS.
- **Nursing**: continuous **telemetry**, **symptom checks**, **transcutaneous pacing pad** readiness when unstable.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **inferior MI** history develops **HR 38**, **BP 78/50**, and **dizziness**. Telemetry shows **more P waves than QRS complexes** with **wide escape rhythm**.

**Fork:** **Unstable bradycardia** — **call for help**, **prepare TCP**, **give atropine** per order/protocol, **oxygen** as indicated, and **continuous monitoring**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Symptoms + high-grade block** = urgent pathway.
- **Mobitz II** is **more ominous** than Wenckebach in many acute settings.

**Related:** [temporary pacemaker care](LESSON:temporary-pacemaker-bedside-nursing) · [ACS](LESSON:acute-coronary-syndrome-nclex-rn).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Ventricular Dysrhythmias: VT, VF, and Torsades
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Ventricular Dysrhythmias: VT, VF, and Torsades | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Ventricular Dysrhythmias: VT, VF, and Torsades — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Ventricular tachycardia (VT)** ranges from **stable monomorphic** rhythms to **pulseless arrest**. **Ventricular fibrillation (VF)** is a **perfusionless** rhythm requiring **defibrillation**. **Torsades de pointes** is **polymorphic VT** in the setting of **QT prolongation** and may respond to **magnesium** while **correcting triggers** (electrolytes, drug toxicity). Nursing roles centre on **early recognition**, **defibrillator readiness**, **CPR quality**, and **post-resuscitation** monitoring.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX-RN uses **pulse check** and **stability** forks. **Pulseless VT/VF** → **defibrillate** and **CPR** per BLS/ACLS training themes. **Stable VT with pulse** → **provider-led pharmacologic cardioversion** and **monitoring** — not unsupervised ambulation.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **VF/pulseless VT**: **immediate defibrillation** + CPR per protocol.
- **Torsades**: **magnesium sulfate** themes; **overdrive pacing** in refractory cases per specialty.
- **QT monitoring** on high-risk meds (multiple antiarrhythmics, some antibiotics, ondansetron caution contexts).
- **Safety**: clear the bed during **shock**; document times and outcomes.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client on **telemetry** suddenly **loses consciousness** with **rapid wide-complex rhythm** and **no palpable pulse**.

**Fork:** **Cardiac arrest pathway** — **call for help**, start **CPR**, **retrieve AED/defibrillator**, and **defibrillate** for shockable rhythm per training.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **No pulse** changes every answer set.
- **Magnesium** belongs in **torsades** teaching, not every wide-complex rhythm.

**Related:** [defibrillation vs cardioversion](LESSON:defibrillation-vs-synchronized-cardioversion-nclex-rn) · [digoxin toxicity](LESSON:digoxin-toxicity-nursing-safety).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Pacemakers and ICDs: Nursing Assessment and Safety
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Pacemakers and ICDs: Nursing Assessment and Safety | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Pacemakers and ICDs: Nursing Assessment and Safety — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Permanent pacemakers** support **rate/bradycardia** indications; **implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs)** also treat **life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias**. Nurses monitor **incision sites**, **device interrogation plans**, **magnet policy** per facility, **arm restrictions**, and **psychosocial** responses to shocks. **Electromagnetic interference** is a teaching topic for home and community exposures.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards test **post-op checks** (pocket hematoma, signs of infection), **patient anxiety after shock**, and **MRI compatibility** themes (follow device card and orders). Avoid advising patients to **disable therapies** without cardiology direction.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Incision**: redness, drainage, pain out of proportion — escalate infection concerns.
- **Shock burden**: repeated shocks → **emergency** evaluation.
- **Activity**: usually **limited** lifting on device side early post-op per orders.
- **Documentation**: capture **symptoms** with **time** of suspected device therapy.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client **24 hours post ICD** reports **three shocks** in an hour with **ongoing dizziness**.

**Fork:** **Treat as emergency** — **monitor**, **establish IV access**, **notify cardiology/rapid response**, and **prepare for interrogation** per policy.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Repeated shocks** are never “watch and wait” at home or on the unit without provider direction.
- **Pocket swelling** can be hematoma or bleed — measure and escalate.

**Related:** [temporary pacemaker](LESSON:temporary-pacemaker-bedside-nursing) · [VT/VF lesson](LESSON:ventricular-dysrhythmias-vt-vf-torsades).

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: A **repeatable ECG sequence** reduces errors: **rate**, **rhythm**, **axis**, **intervals (PR/QRS/QT)**, **ST/T changes**, and **clinical correlation**. NCLEX-RN rewards **identifying ischemia**, **life-threatening arrhythmias**, **hyperkalemia peaks**, and **drug toxicity patterns** — then choosing **assessment and escalation** over charting alone.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items often show **one lead snippet** — practice naming **ST elevation territory** when possible and **differentiating** artifact from ischemia. Traps include **ignoring prolonged QT** or **missing irregularly irregular** rhythm as AFib.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Rate**: 300 method for regular rhythms; **irregular** → count over 6 seconds ×10.
- **Axis deviation** hints: **LBBB/RBBB**, **hemiblocks** — document and notify when new.
- **Ischemia**: ST changes + symptoms → **time-sensitive** pathway.
- **QT**: prolonged → **torsades risk**; review meds and electrolytes per orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A nurse prints a **12-lead** showing **ST elevations** in **inferior leads** with **active chest pain**.

**Fork:** **Activate ACS pathway** per facility — **continuous monitoring**, **notify provider**, **prepare** for labs/orders, **avoid** solo walks to the bathroom.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **System beats guessing** — same order every strip.
- **Symptoms + ECG** together drive urgency.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Aortic Stenosis vs Aortic Regurgitation
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Aortic Stenosis vs Aortic Regurgitation | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Aortic Stenosis vs Aortic Regurgitation — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Aortic stenosis (AS)** obstructs **systolic ejection**, producing a **crescendo–decrescendo systolic murmur** radiating to the **carotids**, **narrow pulse pressure**, and **syncope/angina/HF** symptoms when severe. **Aortic regurgitation (AR)** leaks in **diastole**, yielding a **blowing early diastolic murmur** at the **left sternal border**, **wide pulse pressure**, and **bounding pulses** when hemodynamically significant.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **afterload sensitivity** in AS (avoid **vasodilator hypotension** themes without careful monitoring) versus **volume support** nuances in acute AR presentations when the stem frames **shock** — always follow the vignette’s orders and hemodynamics.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **AS**: **delayed carotid upstroke**, **S4** common; **avoid severe hypotension**.
- **AR**: **Corrigan pulse** themes, **pulsatile nail bed** (Quincke) when described.
- **Nursing**: **orthostatic** symptoms, **activity tolerance**, **infection** prophylaxis teaching for valve disease per current guideline framing in the stem.
- **Post-TAVR** themes may appear as **bleeding** and **conduction checks** — monitor per protocol.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with known **severe AS** receives **hydralazine** for BP and becomes **hypotensive with altered mentation**.

**Fork:** **Perfusion crisis** — **stop** further doses per scope, **assess**, **notify provider**, **prepare** for ordered vasopressors/volume cautiously per AS physiology.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Narrow vs wide pulse pressure** is a quick discriminator on exams.
- **Hypotension + AS** is high risk — communicate early.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Mitral Stenosis vs Mitral Regurgitation
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Mitral Stenosis vs Mitral Regurgitation | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Mitral Stenosis vs Mitral Regurgitation — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Mitral stenosis (MS)** limits **left atrial emptying** into the LV, often from **rheumatic** disease, with **low-pitched diastolic rumble** after an opening snap and **pulmonary congestion** out of proportion to LV systolic function. **Mitral regurgitation (MR)** leaks **systolic** flow back to the LA, causing a **holosystolic murmur** at the **apex** radiating to the axilla and **volume overload** of the LA/LV over time.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards link **AFib + MS** as **stroke risk** and **rate-control** teaching, and **acute MR** (papillary rupture post-MI) as **flash pulmonary edema** emergencies requiring **rapid escalation**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **MS**: **diastolic** problem — think **LA pressure**, **dyspnea with exertion**, **hemoptysis** in severe cases.
- **MR**: **holosystolic**; acute MR from **rupture** → sudden respiratory failure.
- **Nursing**: **telemetry**, **anticoagulation** teaching when AFib coexists, **I&O**, **oxygen**.
- **Post-procedure** mitral clips/repairs → **complication surveillance** per orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post-MI client suddenly develops **frothy pink sputum** and **crackles** with a **new harsh murmur**.

**Fork:** Think **acute valvular catastrophe** — **high-Fowler’s**, **oxygen**, **notify provider**, **prepare** for echo/surgical consult themes — not routine discharge teaching.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Timing within the cardiac cycle** defines murmur type.
- **Sudden MR** is a **respiratory emergency** until proven otherwise.

**Related:** [HF priorities](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy) · [MI troponin](LESSON:acute-myocardial-infarction-troponin).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Murmur Recognition for Nurses (High-Yield Patterns) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Murmur Recognition for Nurses (High-Yield Patterns) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: NCLEX-RN expects **pattern recognition**, not cardiology fellowship detail: **systolic vs diastolic timing**, **location**, **radiation**, and **clinical context** (fever + new murmur → **endocarditis** suspicion; **radiation to carotids** → **AS**). Nurses correlate murmur **changes** with **hemodynamics** and **notify** the provider for **new or worsening** sounds after MI, procedures, or sepsis.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Traps include **confusing a benign flow murmur** with **pathologic murmur** when the stem gives **fever**, **embolic phenomena**, or **acute HF**. Another trap is **delaying notification** to finish routine tasks.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Systolic**: AS, MR, VSD, **HOCM** vignettes.
- **Diastolic**: AR, MS.
- **Continuous**: PDA, sometimes **AV fistula** contexts.
- **Always** pair sound with **vitals, SpO₂, perfusion**.
- **Documentation**: timing, grade if trained, location, patient position.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **IVDU** has a **new murmur** on day 3 of **fever**.

**Fork:** **IE workup** pathway — **blood cultures** per orders before antibiotics when stable enough, **monitor for emboli**, **strict line care**.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **New murmur + fever** → infection/endocarditis differential until cleared.
- **Compare** to prior assessments when possible.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cardiac Catheterization: Pre- and Post-Procedure Nursing Care | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Cardiac Catheterization: Pre- and Post-Procedure Nursing Care — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Cardiac catheterization** diagnoses and treats coronary disease. Pre-procedure nursing focuses on **NPO orders**, **allergy history** (iodinated contrast), **renal baseline**, **medication holds** (metformin themes per protocol), **vascular access site** prep, and **anxiety reduction**. Post-procedure care emphasizes **bedrest**, **distal perfusion checks**, **bleeding/hematoma surveillance**, and **hydration** per orders to protect kidneys.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX punishes **early ambulation** after **femoral** access when orders require flat bedrest. It rewards **recognizing retroperitoneal bleed** (back pain, dropping Hgb, hypotension) and **false aneurysm** signs at the groin.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Radial vs femoral**: **radial** often allows earlier mobility per protocol.
- **Post**: frequent **distal pulses**, **sandbag** themes if used, **pain** at site.
- **Contrast**: **flush kidneys** per orders; monitor **UO**.
- **Teach**: when to call for **bleeding**, **chest pain**, **neurologic changes**.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **femoral cath**, the client develops **increasing groin pain**, **BP drop**, and **pale foot**.

**Fork:** **Vascular emergency** until evaluated — **notify provider**, **mark pulses**, **hold pressure** per protocol, **prepare** for rapid response imaging/surgery themes.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Pulse + site + BP** together beat “mild discomfort.”
- **Contrast + AKI risk** → monitor renal status per orders.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: PCI and Stents: Complications and Nursing Priorities — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)** restores perfusion but carries **bleeding**, **vascular access injury**, **stent thrombosis**, **contrast-induced nephropathy**, and **arrhythmia** risks. Nurses monitor **antiplatelet adherence**, **groin/radial site**, **troponin trends**, **rhythm**, and **chest pain recurrence** — linking symptoms to **urgent re-evaluation**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Stent thrombosis**: sudden pain + ST changes → **emergency** activation.
- **Access site**: swelling, bruit, pulse loss.
- **Renal protection**: fluids/hold nephrotoxins per orders.
- **Medication teaching**: **do not stop DAPT** without cardiology unless bleeding mandates it — per stem.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** 6 hours post **PCI**, the client reports **crushing chest pain** with **ST elevation** on monitor.

**Fork:** **ACS recurrence** — **oxygen** per orders, **notify provider**, **12-lead**, **prepare** for repeat cath lab activation per policy.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Recurrent chest pain post PCI** is treated as **emergent**.
- **Adherence** to antiplatelet plans prevents thrombosis.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Temporary Pacemakers: Bedside Safety and Troubleshooting
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Temporary Pacemakers: Bedside Safety and Troubleshooting | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Temporary Pacemakers: Bedside Safety and Troubleshooting — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Temporary transvenous pacing** supports the heart during **high-grade AV block**, **brady arrest risk**, or **bridge** to permanent device. Nurses maintain **connector integrity**, **threshold checks** per policy, **dressing and insertion site** care, **spike capture on monitor**, and **prevent dislodgement** by securing cables and educating about **movement limits**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items reward **loss of capture** recognition (pacing spikes without following QRS), **battery/generator checks**, and **never** tugging wires. Expect **microshock** prevention themes in older teaching contexts.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Capture**: each spike should **follow** with depolarization when pacing.
- **Sensitivity**: **undersensing** can cause dangerous stacking — escalate per policy.
- **Safety**: **two-person** checks when changing settings unless protocol allows solo RN adjustments.
- **Backup**: **TCP pads** on unstable patients when ordered.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Pacing alarms show **spikes without QRS** and **HR falls to 30**.

**Fork:** **Loss of capture** — **call for help**, **ensure emergency transcutaneous pacing readiness**, **notify provider**, **check connections** per training.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Capture failure** is an **immediate** safety issue.
- **Secure externalized hardware** every shift.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Post Cardiac Surgery Priorities (CABG / Valve)
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Post Cardiac Surgery Priorities (CABG / Valve) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Post Cardiac Surgery Priorities (CABG / Valve) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: After **sternotomy**, priorities include **airway and gas exchange**, **hemorrhage/tamponade** surveillance, **arrhythmia** monitoring (**AFib** common), **pain control** that enables **deep breathing**, **glycemic** control per orders, **early progressive mobility** when safe, and **sternal precautions**. **Cardiac output** is inferred from **MAP, UO, lactate, mixed venous** themes, and **drains/chest tube** output when present.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **mediastinal bleeding** (rising chest tube output, tamponade signs), **hypoxemia** after bypass, and **prioritizing** assessment over routine tasks when drains change abruptly.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Mediastinal bleed**: **sudden** drainage change, **tachycardia**, **narrow pulse pressure** cues.
- **AFib**: **rate control** and **anticoagulation** per orders.
- **Sternal precautions**: **splinting** with cough, **no heavy pushing/pulling** early.
- **Glycemic**: avoid **hyperglycemia** in cardiac surgery populations per protocol.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** CABG post-op hour 4: **chest tube output jumps** to **400 mL in 30 minutes** with **BP falling**.

**Fork:** **Hemorrhage** — **notify surgeon**, **prepare blood** per protocol, **support perfusion** with ordered fluids/pressors, **repeat vitals** frequently.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Acute drain output change + hypotension** = **surgical emergency** pathway.
- **Splinting** supports **gas exchange** and **sternal** protection.

**Related:** [tamponade](LESSON:cardiac-tamponade-nclex-rn) · [AFib](LESSON:atrial-fibrillation-rate-control).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Chronic Venous Insufficiency
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Chronic Venous Insufficiency | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Chronic Venous Insufficiency — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI)** reflects **valve incompetence** and **venous hypertension**, causing **edema**, **heaviness**, **varicosities**, **stasis dermatitis**, and **ulcer risk** at the **gaiter area**. Nursing emphasizes **compression therapy** when not contraindicated, **skin care**, **mobility**, **elevation**, and **differentiating** from **acute DVT**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards test **compression contraindications** (ABI themes when arterial disease coexists) and **avoiding** injudicious **diuretics** when edema is venous rather than cardiogenic unless ordered for comorbid HF.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Edema**: pitting, improves with **elevation**.
- **Skin**: **hemosiderin staining**, **lipodermatosclerosis** descriptions.
- **Ulcer location**: **medial malleolus** classic for venous.
- **Education**: **daily walking**, **weight management**, **leg elevation**, **compression adherence**.
- **DVT symptoms**: unilateral calf pain — **do not massage**; evaluate per protocol.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with CVI has **bilateral ankle edema** but **new left calf redness** and **10/10 pain**.

**Fork:** **Suspect DVT** until evaluated — **notify provider**, **avoid massage**, **compare calves**, **monitor** for PE symptoms.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **New unilateral** pain changes the differential.
- **Compression** requires **arterial adequacy** screening per protocol.

**Related:** [arterial vs venous ulcers](LESSON:arterial-ulcers-vs-venous-ulcers) · [varicose veins](LESSON:varicose-veins-nursing-teaching).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Arterial vs Venous Ulcers
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Arterial vs Venous Ulcers | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Arterial vs Venous Ulcers — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Venous ulcers** usually occur **medially** above the **malleolus**, with **irregular borders**, **moderate exudate**, and surrounding **hemosiderin staining**. **Arterial ulcers** often sit on **toes/heels/shins**, are **punched-out**, **painful** especially when **elevated**, with **pale** bases and **diminished pulses**. Nursing priorities include **perfusion assessment**, **appropriate dressings**, **offloading**, and **referral** patterns.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX punishes **elevating legs** for pure arterial ischemic pain when the stem describes **critical limb ischemia** without clarifying mixed disease — follow **comfort positioning** per orders. It rewards **ABI** and **vascular consult** themes.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Venous**: weeping, **brawny** skin, **improves** with compression when safe.
- **Arterial**: **claudication** history, **hair loss**, **shiny skin**, **dependent rubor** themes.
- **Mixed disease** is common — **individualize** care per orders.
- **Wound photos** and **measurements** for trending.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Ulcer on **lateral fifth toe**, **severe pain at rest**, **ABI 0.45**.

**Fork:** **Arterial insufficiency** — **avoid harmful compression**, **notify provider/vascular**, **foot protection**, **pain control** per orders.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Location + pain + pulses** discriminate faster than size alone.
- **ABI** guides **compression safety**.

**Related:** [CVI](LESSON:chronic-venous-insufficiency-nursing) · [Raynaud](LESSON:raynaud-phenomenon-nursing-care).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Raynaud’s Phenomenon: Triggers and Self-Management
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Raynaud’s Phenomenon: Triggers and Self-Management | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Raynaud’s Phenomenon: Triggers and Self-Management — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Raynaud phenomenon** causes **episodic digital vasospasm** with **tricolor** changes (white → blue → red) triggered by **cold** or **stress**. **Primary** Raynaud is more benign; **secondary** forms associate with **systemic sclerosis**, **lupus**, or **occupational vibration** and need **rheumatology** follow-up. Nursing focuses on **trigger avoidance**, **smoking cessation**, **skin integrity**, and **recognizing** digital ulcers as escalation signs.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items test **patient teaching** (layered clothing, gradual rewarming) versus **medical mismanagement** (rapid extreme heat that could burn numb digits). Secondary disease stems may add **digital pitting scars** or **abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy** themes as context.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Warm gradually**; avoid **burns**.
- **CCBs** are common first-line pharmacologic themes for frequent attacks — per orders.
- **Assess** for **tissue injury** and infection.
- **Work modifications** for vibration exposure when applicable.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **new fingertip ulcers** after winter exposure on **background scleroderma** features in the stem.

**Fork:** **Secondary disease escalation** — **notify provider**, **protect digits**, **document perfusion**, **rheumatology** follow-up themes.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Ulcerated digits** are not “just Raynaud” — investigate **secondary** causes.
- **Smoking cessation** is foundational.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Varicose Veins: Conservative Care and When to Escalate
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Varicose Veins: Conservative Care and When to Escalate | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Varicose Veins: Conservative Care and When to Escalate — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Varicose veins** are **dilated superficial veins** from **valve failure** and **chronic venous hypertension**. Most care is **conservative**: **compression**, **exercise**, **elevation**, **weight management**, and **skin protection**. **Escalation** signs include **thrombophlebitis**, **bleeding** from ruptured varix, **rapidly worsening** pain, or **DVT** suspicion.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **superficial thrombophlebitis** vs **DVT** symptom clusters and **patient education** for **long trips** (movement, hydration, compression when appropriate).

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Conservative**: **graduated compression stockings** when not contraindicated.
- **Procedures**: ablation/stripping teaching includes **post-procedure walking** per orders.
- **Bleeding**: **elevate leg + pressure** for superficial bleeding — still notify provider if significant.
- **Skin**: moisturize to reduce **excoriation** risk.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client’s **varicosity** **bleeds** through a **thin skin tear** after scratching.

**Fork:** **Apply pressure**, **elevate**, **assess** hemodynamic stability, **notify provider** if bleeding persists or client is anticoagulated.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Bleeding varicosities** can be dramatic — **pressure first**.
- **Compression** is preventive and therapeutic when safe.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Antihypertensives: ACE Inhibitors, ARBs, Beta Blockers, and CCBs
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Antihypertensives: ACE Inhibitors, ARBs, Beta Blockers, and CCBs | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Antihypertensives: ACE Inhibitors, ARBs, Beta Blockers, and CCBs — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **ACE inhibitors** and **ARBs** block the **RAAS**; **beta blockers** reduce **HR/contractility**; **calcium channel blockers** split into **dihydropyridines** (mostly vascular) and **non-dihydropyridines** (AV nodal effects). NCLEX-RN stresses **contraindications** (pregnancy for ACE/ARB, asthma caution themes with non-selective beta blockers per stem), **hyperkalemia** with RAAS blockers, and **reflex tachycardia** with vasodilating CCBs.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards love **first-dose hypotension**, **cough/angioedema** with ACE, **avoiding combined ACE+ARB**, and **holding beta blockers** perioperatively per **explicit** order scenarios.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **ACE**: monitor **K⁺/Cr**, **cough**, **angioedema** risk.
- **ARB**: similar labs; **pregnancy** contraindication.
- **Beta blockers**: **bradycardia**, **masks hypoglycemia** in diabetes teaching.
- **CCB**: **peripheral edema** with dihydropyridines; **heart block** risk with diltiazem/verapamil.
- **Teaching**: **orthostatic precautions**, **do not stop abruptly** for BB without orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** After starting **lisinopril**, **K⁺ 5.8** with **wide QRS** and **weakness**.

**Fork:** **Hyperkalemia emergency** pathway per orders — **notify provider**, **obtain repeat ECG**, **prepare** for ordered calcium/insulin/glucose/binder therapies, **stop RAAS** meds per order.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **ACE/ARB + K⁺** trends are a safety pair.
- **BB** stops require **provider guidance**.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Antiarrhythmics Simplified for NCLEX-RN — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: NCLEX-RN focuses on **class effects and toxicities** more than Vaughan-Williams trivia: **amiodarone** (pulmonary/fthyroid/liver/QT), **procainamide** (lupus-like), **flecainide** (structural heart cautions in stems), and **adenosine** (brief asystole, bronchospasm caution). Nurses monitor **BP**, **rhythm**, **QT**, **lungs**, and **thyroid** symptoms on chronic amiodarone per orders.

sections.heading: Exam relevance

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Anticoagulants** (heparin, LMWH, warfarin, DOACs) reduce **clot formation**; **antiplatelets** (aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors) reduce **platelet aggregation**. NCLEX-RN emphasizes **bleeding precautions**, **lab monitoring** (aPTT/anti-Xa/INR as applicable), **reversal agent** themes in overdose/bleed vignettes, and **spinal/epidural** timing with anticoagulation.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards test **therapeutic vs toxic** heparin (bleeding, **thrombocytopenia**), **DOAC** adherence, and **never** double up **NSAIDs** on antiplatelet therapy without orders.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Heparin**: **aPTT** or **anti-Xa** per protocol; **platelet** surveillance.
- **Warfarin**: **INR**; **vitamin K** reversal themes.
- **DOACs**: **renal dosing**; fewer routine labs.
- **Education**: **head injury** urgency, **electric razor**, **fall precautions**.
- **Procedures**: **hold windows** per anesthesia/anticoagulation policies — never guess.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** On **apixaban**, the client **hits their head** with **brief LOC**.

**Fork:** **Emergency evaluation** — **notify provider**, **neuro checks**, **imaging** per protocol, **hold** further doses until cleared — not “sleep it off.”

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Head trauma + anticoagulant** = **high stakes**.
- **Bleeding + hypotension** activates **massive transfusion** themes per orders.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Digoxin** narrows the therapeutic window with **renal impairment**, **hypokalemia**, **hypomagnesemia**, **hypercalcemia**, and **amiodarone interactions**. Toxicity can present as **GI symptoms**, **visual changes** (yellow-green halos in classic teaching), **bradyarrhythmias**, and **ventricular ectopy**. Nurses monitor **apical pulse**, **electrolytes**, **renal function**, and **drug levels** per orders.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **holding** digoxin for **critical bradycardia** or **high risk** until provider review, and **repleting potassium** carefully in **renal failure** contexts per orders.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Hold parameters**: **HR** thresholds per facility/order.
- **Toxicity labs**: **K⁺/Mg²⁺/Cr**; **drug level** when ordered.
- **Antidote**: **Digoxin immune Fab** in severe toxicity per orders.
- **Education**: **consistent timing**, **avoid OTC** potassium-wasting drugs unless approved.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Digoxin patient has **N/V**, **HR 48**, and **bigeminy** on monitor with **K⁺ 2.9**.

**Fork:** **Toxicity suspicion** — **notify provider**, **hold** next dose per order, **prepare** for labs/ECG/monitoring, **avoid** rapid unsupervised potassium replacement.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **GI + rhythm + low K⁺** is a classic cluster.
- **Apical pulse** before each dose when protocol requires.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Cardiogenic Shock
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cardiogenic Shock | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Cardiogenic Shock — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Cardiogenic shock** is **inadequate CO** from **primary pump failure** (large MI, advanced HF, valvular catastrophe) leading to **hypoperfusion** despite adequate intravascular volume or with **congestion** patterns depending on the lesion. Nurses recognize **cool clammy skin**, **oliguria**, **altered mentation**, **lactate elevation**, **hypotension**, and **pulmonary edema** combinations and support **ordered inotropes/pressors**, **IABP/Impella** device themes, and **rapid cath/surgery** activation.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards separate **cardiogenic** shock (fluids often **harmful** unless specifically indicated) from **hypovolemic** shock. Trap answers give **large crystalloid boluses** to a client with **acute pulmonary edema** and **cold shock**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Warm vs cold** shock variants appear in advanced items — still anchor to **pump failure**.
- **Pressors/inotropes**: **titrate** to ordered MAP with **continuous monitoring**.
- **Devices**: **IABP** timing/trigger assessments per ICU protocol.
- **Oxygen/ventilation** support as ordered.
- **I&O** and **labs** trending.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post **STEMI**, **BP 72/50**, **crackles bilaterally**, **UOP 10 mL/hr**, **clammy skin**.

**Fork:** **Cardiogenic shock** — **notify provider**, **high-acuity monitoring**, **prepare** for ordered pressors/inotropes and **urgent revascularization** themes — not **1 L NS bolus** by default.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Cold + wet + hypotension** points to **pump** failure patterns.
- **Fluids** are not the default rescue.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Stages of Shock (Progressive Assessment) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Stages of Shock (Progressive Assessment) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: Classic teaching describes **initial**, **compensatory**, **progressive**, and **refractory** phases with evolving **HR**, **BP**, **respiratory rate**, **skin**, **ment**, and **renal output**. NCLEX-RN rewards **early recognition** when compensation still masks BP, and **interventions** that **restore perfusion** and **treat cause** rather than delaying for routine tasks.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items use **subtle tachycardia** and **restlessness** before hypotension — choose **assessment + escalation** over reassurance.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Initial**: **vasoconstriction**, **tachycardia**, **mild anxiety**.
- **Compensatory**: **narrowed pulse pressure**, **tachypnea**, **cool extremities**.
- **Progressive**: **hypotension**, **oliguria**, **confusion**.
- **Refractory**: **multiorgan failure** despite therapy — maximal support.
- **Nursing**: **frequent vitals**, **labs**, **oxygen**, **two large-bore IVs** when indicated, **notify** early.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post-op client **HR 118**, **RR 24**, **anxious**, **BP still “normal”** but **cap refill 3 seconds**.

**Fork:** **Compensated shock** suspicion — **notify provider**, **increase monitoring**, **review** bleeding/infection, **avoid** sedating for anxiety alone without assessment.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Normal BP** does not rule out shock early.
- **Trends** beat isolated values.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: Compare **distributive** (warm then cold in septic shock progression), **hypovolemic**, **cardiogenic**, and **obstructive** (PE, tamponade, tension pneumo) patterns. **CVP/PAWP** and **echo** themes appear as **provider-interpreted** data — the nurse focuses on **bedside surrogates**: **MAP**, **UOP**, **lactate**, **echoless tamponade** cues, and **ultrasound** call for help patterns.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX punishes **fluids** in **tamponade** or **tension pneumothorax** when the answer should be **procedure** (pericardiocentesis, needle decompression) per training themes.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Septic early**: **warm shock**; late **cold**.
- **Cardiogenic**: **high filling pressures** + **low output** patterns in stems.
- **Obstructive**: **JVD** may be **prominent**; **pulsus paradoxus** in tamponade teaching.
- **Hypovolemic**: **flat neck veins**, **responds** to fluids when hemorrhagic.
- **Nursing**: **oxygen**, **monitoring**, **cause-specific** activation.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** **JVD**, **hypotension**, **muffled heart sounds** after **chest trauma**.

**Fork:** **Tamponade** suspicion — **activate emergency team**, **prepare** for **pericardiocentesis** per protocol, **IV access**, **avoid** large fluid boluses as sole therapy.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Beck triad** cues scream **obstructive** cardiac.
- **Fluids** do not fix **every** low BP.

- **Screening**: **lipid panels** per primary care cadence in the stem.
- **Referrals**: **cardiac rehab** after events when applicable.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning

sections.heading: Exam relevance

- **Muscle**: **mild ache** vs **incapacitating pain** + **dark urine**.
- **Liver**: baseline and **symptom-driven** checks per policy.
- **Pregnancy**: **contraindicated** in classic teaching.
- **Patient questions**: use **non-judgmental** language for adherence barriers.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction for Nursing Practice
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction for Nursing Practice | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction for Nursing Practice — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Primary prevention** reduces first events; **secondary prevention** prevents recurrence after MI/stroke. Nurses operationalize **ABCs**: **antiplatelet/anticoagulation** per indication, **BP control**, **cholesterol management**, **cigarette cessation**, **diet/activity**, **DM control**, and **psychosocial stress** screening. **Cardiac rehab** referral and **vaccination** themes may appear as holistic items.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards reward **prioritizing** unstable symptoms over prevention teaching, but when stable, **SMART goals** and **collaborative** plans win over lecturing.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **BP**: home logs, medication adherence.
- **DM**: **HbA1c** targets per orders.
- **Activity**: gradual progression post-event per rehab.
- **Mental health**: **depression** after MI impacts adherence — screen when stem cues.
- **Community**: **food security** and **access** affect plan feasibility — nurse advocates for resources.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Post-MI client is **stable** but **never fills** metoprolol because of **cost**.

**Fork:** **Barrier assessment** — **notify provider/pharmacy** for alternatives, **teach** assistance programs, **document** adherence plan — not blame.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Non-adherence** often has **fixable** causes.
- **Rehab referral** saves lives when available.

**Related:** [HF](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy) · [PCI teaching](LESSON:pci-stents-complications-nursing-priorities).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Aortic Dissection: Recognition and Priorities
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Aortic Dissection: Recognition and Priorities | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Aortic Dissection: Recognition and Priorities — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Aortic dissection** is a **tear** in the intima creating a **false lumen** with **sudden tearing chest pain** radiating to the **back**, **BP differential** between arms in some types, and **pulse deficits**. Nursing activates **emergency pathways**, **controls BP/pain** per orders (often **beta blockade first** to reduce **dP/dt**), and **prepares** for **imaging** and **surgery**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX punishes **thrombolytics** when **dissection** is suspected with **tearing pain** and **BP differential**. It rewards **two large-bore IVs**, **type-specific BP targets**, and **immediate provider communication**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Pain**: **sudden**, **severe**, **ripping** language.
- **VS**: **arm-to-arm BP** differences, **migrating** pain.
- **Med themes**: **IV beta blocker** before vasodilation in classic teaching — follow stem.
- **Avoid**: **anticoagulation** until dissection excluded when ACS unclear.
- **Post-op**: **stroke** surveillance with **arch** repairs in advanced items.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** **Sudden interscapular pain**, **right BP 190/100**, **left BP 120/70**, **HR 104**.

**Fork:** **Dissection** until proven otherwise — **emergency activation**, **large-bore access**, **notify provider**, **avoid** thrombolysis if stem suggests dissection over STEMI.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **BP differential + tearing pain** is a **red flag** cluster.
- **Speed** matters more than perfect paperwork.

**Related:** [hypertensive crisis](LESSON:hypertensive-crisis-vs-urgency) · [ACS](LESSON:acute-coronary-syndrome-nclex-rn).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Pericarditis vs Cardiac Tamponade
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Pericarditis vs Cardiac Tamponade | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Pericarditis vs Cardiac Tamponade — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Pericarditis** inflames the pericardium with **pleuritic positional pain** and often **diffuse ST elevation** with **PR depression**. **Tamponade** is **fluid under pressure** impairing **ventricular filling**, producing **hypotension**, **JVD**, **muffled heart sounds**, and **pulsus paradoxus** themes. Nurses trend **vitals**, **ECG**, and **echo** orders while watching for **transition** from pericarditis to **effusion/tamponade**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **when NSAIDs are appropriate** versus **when pericardiocentesis** is the priority. **Hypotension + rising JVD** shifts answers away from “more ibuprofen.”

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Pericarditis**: **pain improves leaning forward**; **friction rub** may wax/wane.
- **Tamponade**: **Beck triad** cues; **tachycardia** out of proportion.
- **Nursing**: **serial vitals**, **prepare** for **pericardiocentesis** tray per ICU policy, **IV access**.
- **Anticoagulation** caution when **large effusion** in stems — follow orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Known **pericarditis** now **hypotensive**, **JVD to earlobe**, **HR 130**.

**Fork:** **Tamponade physiology** — **emergency team**, **echo**, **pericardiocentesis** per orders — not discharge teaching.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Pericarditis can evolve** — reassess frequently.
- **Hypotension + distended neck veins** = **obstructive** emergency until cleared.

**Related:** [pericarditis ECG](LESSON:pericarditis-ecg-clues) · [tamponade](LESSON:cardiac-tamponade-nclex-rn).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Chest Pain: What Do You Do First?
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Chest Pain: What Do You Do First? | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Chest Pain: What Do You Do First? — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: This lesson trains **NGN-style sequencing**: **assess airway and breathing effort**, **obtain vitals and SpO₂**, **apply oxygen when hypoxemic per orders**, **obtain/verify a 12-lead ECG when ordered**, **establish IV access**, and **notify the provider** with **concise data** — before **routine meds**, **meals**, or **ambulation**. The goal is to **rule in life threats** (ACS, PE, dissection, pneumothorax) while **preventing harm**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Traps include **ambulating** for “comfort,” **giving nitroglycerin** without **BP context**, or **teaching** before **stabilizing** unstable vitals.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **First**: **assess** + **monitor** + **communicate**.
- **Hypoxia** → **oxygen** per protocol.
- **ACS suspicion** → **aspirin** only when **not contraindicated** per orders and scope.
- **Females and diabetes** may have **atypical** pain — still pursue risk.
- **Document** times for **door-to-ECG** style quality themes when shown.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Client reports **new chest pressure** with **SpO₂ 88%** on room air.

**Fork:** **Oxygen + assessment + monitoring + notify provider + prepare ECG** — not “finish breakfast tray.”

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Airway–breathing–circulation** logic still wins on boards.
- **Objective data first**, then targeted interventions.

**Related:** [ACS](LESSON:acute-coronary-syndrome-nclex-rn) · [dissection](LESSON:aortic-dissection-priorities-nclex-rn).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Post PCI Complications: Who Do You See First?
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Post PCI Complications: Who Do You See First? | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Post PCI Complications: Who Do You See First? — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: After **PCI**, prioritize clients by **instability**: **reperfusion arrhythmias**, **access site bleeding with hypotension**, **acute stent thrombosis** (recurrent ischemic pain + ST changes), and **contrast reactions** outrank **routine pain** or **scheduled vitals** on stable neighbors. Nurses use **objective triggers** (BP, rhythm, site, UO) to **sequence** assessments in **multi-patient** stems.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NGN **matrix** items reward **one unstable client** among three stable ones — choose **bleeding with dropping BP** or **recurrent ST elevation** over **mild groin soreness**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Bleeding + hypotension** → **vascular emergency** pathway.
- **Recurrent chest pain + ST changes** → **repeat activation**.
- **Contrast allergy** → **airway** first.
- **Stable scheduled meds** can wait with **charge nurse** communication when reprioritizing.
- **Delegate** only tasks safe for **UAP** — not unstable assessments.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** You have **three clients**: (A) **groin oozing** with **BP 88/50**, (B) **5/10 groin pain** stable BP, (C) **routine metoprolol** due.

**Fork:** **See Client A first** — **hypotension + bleeding** risk.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Hemodynamic threat** beats **routine** tasks.
- **Communicate** when you **reprioritize** the team workload.

**Related:** [PCI lesson](LESSON:pci-stents-complications-nursing-priorities) · [unstable patient recognition](LESSON:unstable-cardiac-patient-recognition-ngn).

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Which Cardiac Patient Is Unstable? (NGN Judgment)
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Which Cardiac Patient Is Unstable? (NGN Judgment) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Which Cardiac Patient Is Unstable? (NGN Judgment) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: Stability is defined by **perfusion + work of breathing + rhythm risk + trend**, not a single “normal” number. **Red flags** include **new confusion**, **rising lactate**, **narrow pulse pressure**, **cold clammy skin**, **crackles with hypoxia**, **recurrent ventricular ectopy**, **ST changes**, and **access site hemorrhage**. This lesson builds a **checklist** you can apply in **matrix drag-and-drop** and **bow-tie** style items.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners hide instability behind **polite patients** or **normal SpO₂ on minimal exertion** while **work of breathing** is rising. Another trap is **choosing education** for the client who is **mentating off** with **new arrhythmia**.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **ABC + mentation + urine + skin** every time.
- **Compare** to **baseline** when provided.
- **If two answers seem cardiac**, pick the one that **secures monitoring** and **closes the highest-risk gap**.
- **Delegate** stable tasks; **retain** unstable assessments.
- **Escalate** with **SBAR** when thresholds crossed.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** Four clients: **AFib RVR with BP 82/50**, **stable post-cath**, **HF with 1 kg gain**, **chest pain resolved after nitro**.

**Fork:** **AFib RVR with hypotension** is **most unstable** — **assess + notify + prepare cardioversion** per orders/policy.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Hypotension + tachyarrhythmia** usually beats **stable resolved pain**.
- **Trend** mild HF weight when no acute distress.

**Related:** [AFib](LESSON:atrial-fibrillation-rate-control) · [chest pain first actions](LESSON:chest-pain-first-nursing-action-nclex).

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Hemodynamic Monitoring Basics: CVP, MAP, PAWP — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Central venous pressure (CVP)**, **mean arterial pressure (MAP)**, and **pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP)** help nurses interpret **preload**, **afterload**, and **pump function** in critically ill clients. CVP approximates **right-sided filling** when measured at end-expiration with a calibrated transducer; MAP estimates **organ perfusion pressure** and is a common resuscitation target; PAWP reflects **left-sided filling pressures** when a pulmonary artery catheter is in place and the balloon is wedged per protocol. Canadian stems may show **kPa** for pressures in some contexts, but NCLEX-RN prioritization hinges on **trend interpretation**, **artifact avoidance**, and **knowing what each number cannot tell you alone**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Items reward **trending** over single snapshots, **levelling and zeroing** themes, and **first actions** when a line disconnects or dampens. Traps include treating CVP as a perfect volume number, ignoring **valve disease** or **ventricular interdependence**, or choosing **large fluid boluses** solely because CVP is “low” when the client has **end-stage right heart failure** or **tamponade** physiology in the stem.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **CVP**: influenced by **PEEP**, **intra-abdominal pressure**, **tricuspid disease**, and **respiratory variation** — interpret with the whole exam.
- **MAP**: roughly **(SBP + 2×DBP)/3**; used with **urine output** and **mentation** to judge perfusion.
- **PAWP**: obtained only with **provider orders** and **waveform confirmation**; never inflate the balloon longer than protocol allows.
- **Nursing priorities**: maintain **sterile dressings**, **stopcock integrity**, **alarm limits**, and **clear handoff** of numeric baselines after repositioning.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 67-year-old client post–**mitral valve repair** has a **PA catheter** and arterial line. After turning, the nurse notices the **arterial waveform is dampened** and **MAP** reads **58 mmHg**, while **CVP** rose from **8 to 14 mmHg** with unchanged **PAWP** on the screen capture you are shown.

**Fork:** Your **first** actions cluster around **assessing line patency**, **re-levelling** transducers if indicated, **comparing** to non-invasive BP, and **notifying** the provider with **objective data** — not bolusing fluid based on one CVP value alone.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Trend beats snapshot** for all invasive pressures.
- **Waveform quality** is part of assessment — dampening can hide real hypotension.
- **Synthesis:** Pair invasive numbers with **perfusion** (mentation, UO, lactate themes when shown) before choosing aggressive fluids.

**Related:** [heart failure priorities](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy) · [shock recognition](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Arterial Lines and Blood Pressure Monitoring — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Arterial lines** provide **continuous beat-to-beat** blood pressure monitoring and **reliable sampling** for **ABGs** in unstable clients. Nursing responsibilities include **insertion assist** per scope, **securement**, **dressing integrity**, **zeroing** the transducer at the **phlebostatic axis**, **alarm management**, and **recognizing complications** (ischemia, thrombosis, infection, hemorrhage from disconnect). For NCLEX-RN, distinguish **artifact** (kinked tubing, air bubbles, patient movement) from **true hypotension** before escalating therapy.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Boards test **first-line safety**: **stop bleeding** if a line disconnects, **activate help** for sudden hemorrhage, **compare** invasive and non-invasive pressures when waveforms look wrong, and **avoid** wrist flexion injury in radial sites. Expect questions on **Allen test** themes when radial access is planned and **neurovascular checks** for distal perfusion after placement.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Monitoring bundle**: continuous waveform when ordered, **q-shift** site checks, **mark insertion** date, **closed** sampling system.
- **Complications**: **distal ischemia** (pallor, pain, weak pulse), **infection** at hub/dressing, **hematoma**.
- **Overdamped vs underdamped** waveforms change numeric display — reassess setup before treating numbers.
- **Scope:** nurses **do not** independently remove arterial lines unless protocol explicitly allows; follow orders for discontinuation.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 54-year-old client in **ICU** has a **radial arterial line**. The nurse notes **bright red blood** pulsing from a **loose connection** at the stopcock while the monitor alarms **MAP 40**.

**Fork:** **Immediate patient safety** — **tighten/close** the system per training, **apply pressure** if external bleeding, **call for help**, and **notify** the provider while monitoring **distal perfusion**. Do not leave to **complete charting** first.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Disconnect = bleeding risk** until proven otherwise.
- **Waveform troubleshooting** precedes blind fluid pushes.

**Related:** [hemodynamic monitoring](LESSON:hemodynamic-monitoring-cvp-map-pawp) · [shock](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [US hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Cardiac Output and Stroke Volume — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Cardiac output (CO)** is **stroke volume × heart rate** and represents **minute delivery** of blood to tissues. **Stroke volume** depends on **preload**, **afterload**, and **contractility**. NCLEX-RN frames these relationships for **heart failure**, **shock**, and **arrhythmia** vignettes where the nurse must connect **tachycardia** compensating for low SV, or **bradycardia** collapsing CO despite “normal” squeeze.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: Examiners love **compensation limits**: HR can only maintain CO until **diastolic filling** or **ischemia** breaks the cycle. They test **assessment cues** (JVD, lung congestion, cool extremities) rather than asking you to solve thermodilution arithmetic.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Preload**: volume returning to the ventricle — **too low** → hypoperfusion; **too high** → congestion in HF.
- **Afterload**: resistance the ventricle must overcome — **high SVR** can drop SV when contractility is fixed.
- **Contractility**: weakened in **ischemia**, **sepsis late**, **negative inotropes**.
- **Nursing actions**: strict **I&O**, **daily weights**, **telemetry**, **orthostatic vitals** when appropriate, and **medication timing** for afterload reducers per orders.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client with **acute HF** has **HR 128** in **sinus tachycardia**, **BP 92/60**, and **cool fingers** with **UOP 15 mL/hr** for two hours.

**Fork:** Think **low perfusion with compensatory tachycardia** — prioritize **assessment**, **notify provider**, **prepare** for ordered diuretics/inotropes/nitrates per pathway, and **avoid** strenuous ambulation.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **HR is not “just anxiety”** when perfusion is failing.
- **SV × HR** helps you explain why **new AFib** can crash CO.

**Related:** [AFib rate control](LESSON:atrial-fibrillation-rate-control) · [HF priorities](LESSON:heart-failure-nursing-priorities-hy).

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂)
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂) | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NCLEX-RN cardiovascular review: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO₂ / ScvO₂) — nursing assessment, hemodynamic and rhythm safety, prioritization, Canadian- and US-friendly units, and clinical judgment drills.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body: **Mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO₂)** reflects the **balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption** in the pulmonary artery sample. **Central venous ScvO₂** (from a central line) is a related surrogate used in some sepsis bundles. A **falling** value can signal **low delivery** (anemia, hypoxia, low CO) or **high consumption** (fever, shivering, stress) — the nurse integrates **SpO₂**, **hemoglobin**, **perfusion**, and **lactate** themes when the stem provides them.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body: NCLEX tests **interpretation**, not memorizing a single threshold for every device. Expect **pre-analytic** errors (draw from wrong port) and **clinical integration** questions where **SaO₂ is high** but **delivery is still poor** because hemoglobin or CO is inadequate.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
- **Delivery components**: **SaO₂**, **Hb**, **cardiac output**.
- **Consumption**: sepsis, pain, fever — can lower saturation of venous blood even when lungs look “fine.”
- **Nursing care**: label **line ports**, draw from **correct lumens**, trend values, and communicate **sudden** drops.
- **Safety:** never treat a number in isolation — **examine the patient**.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** After **major surgery**, a client’s **ScvO₂** drops from **74% to 62%** with **rising lactate**, **HR 118**, and **BP trending down**.

**Fork:** Treat as **supply/demand mismatch** — **notify provider**, **increase monitoring**, **review** bleeding, **fluid responsiveness** per orders, and **oxygen** therapy per protocol.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Falling SvO₂/ScvO₂ + lactate** is an escalation flag.
- **High SpO₂** does not guarantee adequate **delivery**.

**Related:** [shock recognition](LESSON:shock-recognition-fluids) · [sepsis gold](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-gold).
